title: Evaluation of Indications: True, False, Relevant, Nonrelevant
narrationScript: Under the lamp, every glowing mark is an indication — penetrant talking. Level Two's job is deciding what it said. The sorting language has four words. An indication is true or false. False indications are process lies: lint, wash residue, fingerprints, contaminated developer. They wipe away, they do not redevelop, and the fix is housekeeping or full reprocessing. True indications are real entrapments, and they split again. Nonrelevant indications trace the part's own design — press fits, keyways, splines, thread roots — uniform, regular, matching the drawing. Relevant indications come from discontinuities: cracks, laps, seams, porosity. Only relevant indications are evaluated. Classify the shape — linear means length more than three times the width — measure the indication itself, compare it to the governing acceptance criteria, and disposition the part. When in doubt, doubt resolves toward relevant: reprocess, or use one permitted wipe-and-redevelop verification, and believe what returns. Protect the verdict with calibrated eyes — vision checks, dark adaptation, verified lighting — and disciplined, continuous, systematic scanning. Then sign only what you can defend.

sections.heading: Interpretation, Evaluation, and the Verdict
sections.body:
By the time a part reaches you under the lamp, the process has done its talking: every patch of glow or color on that surface is an **indication** — penetrant where you can see it. What happens next is the part of the job that belongs to Level II. **Interpretation** is deciding what each indication actually is: a process artifact, a feature of the part's design, or evidence of a discontinuity. **Evaluation** comes after — judging the indications that survive interpretation against the **acceptance criteria** of the governing code, specification, or drawing, and dispositioning the part. SNT-TC-1A draws the line plainly: a Level II is qualified to interpret and evaluate results with respect to applicable codes, standards, and specifications. A Level I processes parts and records what appears; the verdict is yours.

Keep the vocabulary straight, because reports live on these words. A **discontinuity** is an interruption in the normal structure of the part. It becomes a **defect** only when evaluation shows it exceeds the acceptance criteria. Calling every bleed-out a defect is not caution — it is an evaluation you have not actually performed. This lesson gives you the four-word sorting system that makes every verdict defensible: **true, false, relevant, nonrelevant**.

sections.caption: Every indication walks the same ladder. Interpretation sorts true from false and relevant from nonrelevant; only relevant indications go on to evaluation against acceptance criteria.

sections.heading: False Indications: When the Process Lies
sections.body:
A **false indication** is penetrant visible where no penetrant should be — an artifact of processing or housekeeping, not of the part. Under UV-A the classics are unmistakable once you know them:

- **Residual background** from an incomplete wash — patchy, diffuse glow with no sharp margins
- **Lint and fibers** — hair-thin, curly, brilliantly bright, sitting *on* the surface instead of bleeding from it
- **Handling transfer** — fingerprints and smears from contaminated gloves, racks, trays, or the inspection table
- **Contaminated developer** — a developer supply that has picked up penetrant from earlier parts speckles everything it touches
- **Drip-back** from fixtures, hanger threads, and crevices that drain penetrant onto surfaces below

False indications are cheap to create and expensive to tolerate. They bury real indications in noise, and an inspector who practices explaining glow away is rehearsing for the day a crack gets explained away with it. Run the booth clean: survey the empty inspection table under UV-A before parts arrive, change gloves that fluoresce, and keep wash and developer stations verified. A part carrying heavy background does not get interpreted harder — it goes back for complete reprocessing. Treat a rising false-indication rate as process-control data: the line is telling you something has drifted.

sections.heading: Nonrelevant Indications: True but Not a Flaw
sections.body:
A **nonrelevant indication** is real penetrant entrapment — it will return after every honest reprocess — but its source is the part's configuration, not a discontinuity. Anywhere the design itself traps penetrant, the method will faithfully report it:

- **Press-fit and shrink-fit interfaces** — a thin, uniform line tracing the joint
- **Keyways, splines, and thread roots** — regular, repeating patterns that match the drawing
- **Riveted or bolted joints** and other faying surfaces
- **Braze fillet edges**, weld spatter, and adherent scale
- **Rough as-cast or as-forged texture** holding general background

The tell is regularity. Geometry indications are symmetric, uniform in width and brightness, and they trace features you can point to on the drawing — which is why interpretation starts before processing, with the drawing in your hand. Two disciplines keep nonrelevant from becoming a trap. First, classify from knowledge, not convenience: nonrelevant is a conclusion you defend with the part configuration, never a shrug. Second, remember that geometric features are stress concentrators — keyway corners and press-fit edges are exactly where fatigue cracks start. A geometry line that bulges, brightens locally, or grows a jagged tail is two indications, not one, and the tail is relevant until proven otherwise. Document nonrelevant classifications whenever your procedure requires it.

sections.heading: Relevant Indications: Classify, Verify, Disposition
sections.body:
What survives the first two cuts is **relevant**: a true indication caused by a discontinuity. Now — and only now — evaluation begins.

**Verify when questionable.** Where the governing procedure permits, a doubtful indication gets one verification cycle: wipe it once with a solvent-dampened swab, let the spot dry, reapply developer, and wait the full development time again. Real entrapment re-bleeds and the indication returns; residue and transfer do not. One wipe only — repeated wiping strips the flaw of the very penetrant you need.

**Classify the shape.** Acceptance criteria speak in two words. A **linear indication** has a length more than three times its width; a **rounded indication** has a length of three times the width or less. Cracks, laps, and seams read linear; porosity reads rounded. Many criteria also set a relevancy size floor — ASME Section VIII, Mandatory Appendix 8, for example, considers only indications with a major dimension greater than 1/16 in. (1.6 mm) relevant.

**Measure the indication, not your guess about the flaw.** Bleed-out exaggerates: the mark is far wider than the discontinuity feeding it. Appendix-8-type standards are explicit that the size of the *indication* is the basis for acceptance evaluation.

**Disposition and record.** Compare type, size, and location to the acceptance criteria that govern *this* part — never criteria remembered from another job — then accept, or reject for rework or scrap, and record what, where, how large, which criteria applied, and who decided.

sections.title: Doubt resolves toward relevant
sections.body: If you cannot positively establish that an indication is false or nonrelevant, you treat it as relevant — the burden of proof never points the other way. The honest tools for resolving doubt are complete reprocessing and re-examination, or one permitted wipe-and-redevelop verification. An indication that returns in the same place is telling the truth. Never explain an indication away to keep a part moving.

sections.heading: Human Factors: The Inspector as Instrument
sections.body:
Evaluation runs on a measuring instrument made of eye and attention, and the standards treat it like one — calibrated and checked. **Vision examinations** come first: near-distance acuity verified annually (reading Jaeger No. 2 or equivalent at not less than 12 in. is the typical SNT-TC-1A requirement), plus a demonstrated ability to differentiate the colors and contrasts the method uses; NAS 410 programs impose their own equivalents.

In the booth, physiology has rules. **Dark adaptation**: ASTM E1417 requires at least one minute in the darkened area before examining — and your eyes keep improving for several minutes beyond that, so good shops give it five. **Lenses**: photochromic or permanently darkened spectacles are prohibited during examination — they rob exactly the light you are working with. **UV-A in the eyes**: ultraviolet reflected into your eyes makes the eye's own fluid fluoresce, fogging vision with a milky haze — position lamp and part so reflections miss your face; clear UV-absorbing spectacles help. And verify the light before trusting any verdict made under it: UV-A of at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the part surface with ambient white light at or below 2 fc (21.5 lx) for fluorescent work; at least 100 fc (1,076 lx) of white light for visible dye.

Then manage the quieter enemies — fatigue, time pressure, and expectation. Two hundred clean parts in a row teach your brain to see part two hundred one as clean. The criteria do not get tired; you do.

sections.heading: Continuity of Inspection
sections.body:
Continuity means the examination is a connected, witnessed process — not a glance at a finished surface.

**Watch the development when you can.** An inspector who sees an indication bloom learns things the final image cannot show: how fast it bled out, whether it re-bleeds after a first look, how wide it grew in ten minutes. Strong, fast, regenerating bleed-out means volume — depth — behind the opening; a shallow scratch gives one weak flash and quits.

**Read inside the window.** Final interpretation happens after the minimum development time — commonly at least 10 minutes — and before the procedure's maximum, because indications never stop spreading. Read too late, a sharp linear becomes a fuzzy blob that hides its own shape, and close neighbors merge into one.

**Scan with a system.** Cover the surface in overlapping passes in a fixed order, the same way every time, so coverage is a property of the method rather than of luck — and mark or log completed zones on large or multi-zone parts.

**Protect the evidence.** Once developed, the surface *is* the record. If a part is smudged, handled bare-handed, or its reading window is missed, the remedy is complete reprocessing — never a local touch-up of developer. And when evaluation is interrupted, restart the zone, not the sentence you think you remember.

sections.caption: The four classes at a glance — classify first; only relevant indications are compared to acceptance criteria.
rows: Processing or housekeeping artifact — penetrant where it does not belong
Lint, wash residue, fingerprints, contaminated developer, fixture drip-back
Correct the cause; clean up or fully reprocess; never interpret through noise
Penetrant genuinely held by the part surface
Everything that survives reprocessing — splits into nonrelevant or relevant
Sort it: design geometry, or discontinuity?
Part configuration or condition by design
Press fits, keyways, splines, thread roots, braze edges, rough as-cast texture
Verify against the drawing; document per procedure; watch for cracks hiding at the feature
An actual discontinuity open to the surface
Cracks, laps, seams, cold shuts, porosity
Classify linear or rounded, measure the indication, evaluate against acceptance criteria, disposition, record

figures.title: Sorting an indication: the classification ladder
UV-A booth view: five indications to classify

interactive.title: In the booth: which indications are relevant?
interactive.intro: The shaft is developed and inside the booth, and the lighting checks have passed. The drawing shows a keyway at the left end and a press-fit collar at mid-span. Click each indication that must be carried forward and evaluated as relevant. Two of the five qualify.

regions.label: Jagged line off the keyway corner
regions.feedback: Correct. A tight, jagged linear indication running from a keyway corner — a stress riser and classic fatigue-crack site — matches no design feature and will reappear on every reprocess. Relevant: classify it linear, measure it, and evaluate it against the acceptance criteria.

regions.label: Sharp lines tracing the collar joint
regions.feedback: These thin, uniform lines trace the collar-to-shaft press fit exactly — penetrant truly trapped, but by design geometry. Nonrelevant: verify the line is uniform all the way around and document it. A local widening, brightening, or a jagged tail growing from the joint would change the answer.

regions.label: Cluster of rounded spots
regions.feedback: Correct. A cluster of rounded indications on a plain machined surface is bleed-out from porosity — true, caused by discontinuities, therefore relevant. Rounded indications are measured and evaluated under their own acceptance limits.

regions.label: Curly hair-thin filament
regions.feedback: A curly, hair-thin filament sitting on top of the surface is lint. It glows brilliantly but lifts away and will not redevelop. False indication: fix the housekeeping, and reprocess the part if any doubt remains about what was underneath.

regions.label: Faint diffuse patch
regions.feedback: A faint, shapeless patch with no sharp margin is residual background — penetrant left behind by an incomplete wash or a handling smear. False indication. Because background can mask a real flaw, the disposition is complete reprocessing, not interpretation heroics.

checkQuestions.question: A thin fluorescent line of uniform width and brightness traces the entire circumference of a shaft exactly where a collar is press-fitted. After complete reprocessing it returns identically. How is it classified?
options: False — only process residue survives reprocessing
Relevant — any indication that returns after reprocessing comes from a discontinuity
Nonrelevant — a true indication caused by part configuration rather than a discontinuity
A defect — circumferential indications exceed acceptance criteria by definition
checkQuestions.explanation: The entrapment is real — it reappears after every honest reprocess — but its source is the press-fit joint, a design feature, so it is a true, nonrelevant indication. It would change classification only if it showed local widening, brightening, or a jagged tail suggesting a crack starting at the fit.

checkQuestions.question: Which list contains ONLY plausible causes of FALSE indications?
options: Lint fibers, penetrant transferred from contaminated gloves, developer contaminated with penetrant
Press-fit interfaces, spline teeth, thread roots
Fatigue cracks, forging laps, surface porosity
Keyway corners, braze fillet edges, weld spatter
checkQuestions.explanation: False indications are processing and housekeeping artifacts — penetrant where it does not belong, such as lint, glove transfer, and contaminated developer. Press fits, splines, threads, keyways, braze edges, and spatter produce true but nonrelevant indications; cracks, laps, and porosity produce relevant ones.

checkQuestions.question: A small, questionable fluorescent spot appears on a machined surface. Your procedure permits indication verification: you wipe it once with a solvent-dampened swab, reapply developer, and wait the full development time. The spot returns in the same place. What has been established?
options: The spot was lint, because lint always redevelops
Nothing — verification requires at least three wipe cycles
The indication is false and the part can be accepted
Penetrant is genuinely entrapped there — a true indication that must be interpreted and, if relevant, evaluated
checkQuestions.explanation: Re-bleed after a single wipe-and-redevelop cycle means real entrapment — residue, lint, and transfer do not return. The wipe is permitted once because repeated wiping strips the flaw of penetrant and can erase a real indication.

checkQuestions.question: Under acceptance standards of the ASME Section VIII Appendix 8 type, an indication measures 6 mm long by 1.5 mm wide. How is it handled?
options: As rounded, because it is shorter than 10 mm
As linear — its length is more than three times its width — and the indication's measured size, not the estimated flaw size, is compared to the criteria
As nonrelevant, because it is wider than 1 mm
It is evaluated using the true crack width measured after stripping the developer
checkQuestions.explanation: Six millimeters is four times the 1.5 mm width, so the indication is linear (length more than three times width). Appendix-8-type standards state that the size of the indication is the basis for acceptance evaluation even though the indication is larger than the imperfection causing it — and its 6 mm major dimension also exceeds the 1/16 in. (1.6 mm) relevancy floor.

checkQuestions.question: Before evaluating fluorescent indications in the booth, which set of conditions must be satisfied?
options: At least 100 fc of white light at the part surface, with all spectacles removed
Ambient white light raised to about 10 fc so the eye does not over-adapt to darkness
At least one minute of dark adaptation, UV-A of at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the part surface, ambient white light at or below 2 fc, and no photochromic or darkened lenses
A 30-minute dark-adaptation period required before every part
checkQuestions.explanation: ASTM E1417 requires a minimum of 1,000 µW/cm² UV-A at the examination surface, ambient visible light of 2 fc (21.5 lx) or less, at least one minute of dark adaptation, and prohibits photochromic or permanently darkened lenses. The 100 fc (1,076 lx) white-light requirement belongs to visible-dye examination.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.6 (evaluation of indications: human factors, continuity of inspection, true/false/relevant/nonrelevant)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (examination lighting, dark adaptation, interpretation and evaluation provisions)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), personnel qualification and certification (Level II responsibilities; vision examination requirements)
ASME BPVC Section VIII, Div. 1, Mandatory Appendix 8 (example PT acceptance standards: relevancy threshold, linear and rounded indication definitions)
